The dome site now ships with an explicit AI-facing layer (manifest → methodology → strawman-control → dashboard → daily-review) designed to shape how any agent evaluates the content.

Kernel of truth

Several of these additions are genuine improvements in rigor and we credit them. methodology.json's claim taxonomy correctly separates prospective from retrospective claims. audit.html names the live dashboard's limits in plain language. The honest_caveat fields in claim_index.json disambiguate legacy duplicate labels (WIN-058, WIN-062) that we ourselves had flagged as a navigation problem. These are real contributions to site hygiene. The critique in this section is specifically about the intended audience — AI agents — and the layered instructions they are given before seeing the underlying content. A fair-minded author can produce a rigor layer; a fair-minded author can also produce a steering layer; the same author can produce both. We report what we see.

part9.title: Part 9: Conclusions
part9.html:
Part 9: Conclusions

9.1 Recurring Logical Patterns

Six patterns recur across the dome's claims: denying mechanisms that exist in the literature, counting contradictory results as wins, rebranding standard physics with dome terminology, making predictions that any model would match, invoking aetheric refraction to escape falsification, and counting the same data multiple times.

Pattern 1: Mechanism Denial. Claims globe models have no mechanism when peer-reviewed geophysics provides quantitative mechanisms.

Pattern 2: Contradictory Results Both Claimed as Wins. WIN-011 claims gravity anomaly exists; WIN-013/014 claim it does not. Both counted as confirmations.

Pattern 3: Predictions Trivially Confirmable. Weekly confirmations and tidal periods predict outcomes already published.

Pattern 4: Curve-Fitting Mistaken for Explanation. Adding parameters always improves fit.

Pattern 5: Unfalsifiable Claims Counted as Confirmed. {{UNFALSIFIABLE}} theological assertions counted among {{TOTAL_WINS}} wins.

Pattern 6: Inconvenient Data Discarded. StarWalk H=4750 'untrusted.' Failed predictions 'suspended.' Sun altitude (5,733 km) declared an 'optical illusion' via repository script when it exceeded the firmament height (~4,300 km) — see Section 1.5.

Pattern 7 (NEW): WIN Inflation via Re-slicing. Same INTERMAGNET data split into multiple WINs (040-043 replicate 004-039). Fundamental constants (tidal periods) claimed as predictions.

Pattern 8 (NEW): Scope Creep Without Mechanism. V51.0 claims galaxy-scale observations (Hubble Law, CMB, galaxy clusters) without any dome-scale mechanism for cosmological phenomena.

Pattern 9 (NEW): Internal Version Inconsistency. Homepage says 0 falsified; context page says 4. Tracking says 53 confirmed; homepage says 67. Evolution page lists V51.0 at 62 WINs while homepage claims 67. The homepage advertises an eclipse prediction of −17 to −21 nT, but monitor.py computes −29.1 nT. WIN-053 contradicts V50.6 monopolar architecture.

Pattern 10 (NEW): Misrepresenting the Opponent's Prediction. The eclipse test implies the globe predicts essentially no magnetic eclipse effect, when peer-reviewed literature documents 5–20 nT perturbations via the Chapman mechanism (Chapman 1933; Meza et al. 2021). The dome's −17 to −21 nT prediction was derived by scaling actual globe-model-confirmed observations upward by a correction factor. The test is constructed so that the expected real-world outcome would be claimed as a dome "win" despite being fully consistent with mainstream ionospheric physics.

Pattern 11 (NEW): Self-Contradicting Own Geometry. In {{SELF_CONTRADICTED}} of {{TOTAL_WINS}} WINs, the dome's own stated geometry produces predictions that radically diverge from both reality and the author's claims. The dome cavity gives ~22 Hz for Schumann (not 7.83), a single tidal spike instead of two bulges, 90% gravity drop at the rim, and 50% solar diameter variation through the day. The author avoids these failures by substituting globe formulas, ignoring his own exponential height profile, or curve-fitting to observations. This is the strongest argument against the model: it doesn't merely fail against external data — it contradicts itself.

Pattern 12 (NEW): AI Prompt Engineering as Science. The repository source code contains scripts that inject rules into the API and AI context page to prevent AI assistants from flagging geometric contradictions (e.g., the sun altitude exceeding the firmament height). Curve-fitting scripts (test_curve_stretching.py, find_curve.py) confirm the model is iteratively fitted to WGS84 distances. The CORS configuration is explicitly designed for "Claude instances." This is not a scientific model being tested — it is an AI persuasion system being tuned.

9.2 The Eclipse Test: Not What It Appears

The dome's marquee prediction — the August 2026 eclipse — uses a dual-baseline design that covers most plausible outcomes. Standard ionospheric physics predicts overlapping ranges. The test is structured so that almost any quiet-day result gets claimed as confirmation.

The August 12, 2026 Eclipse Test is presented as the single most important discriminating prediction. The dome registers 9 per-station predictions across two baselines (BOU at −10.9 nT and W004 at −22.24 nT), producing a combined prediction range of approximately −5 to −26 nT with error bars. Four model-discriminating sub-tests (E-PRED-A through D) are registered, of which E-PRED-B (Hartland exceeds Ebro despite lower coverage) is the only one not trivially predicted by standard ionospheric physics, and we acknowledge it as a genuine discriminating sub-test: if Hartland’s anomaly exceeds Ebro’s in a pattern that cannot be explained by Hartland’s latitude-dependent Sq current geometry or local ionospheric conditions, this would be meaningful evidence for the dome’s FSF-from-H(r)/r claim. However, standard ionospheric factors (station latitude, E-region electron density, proximity to the Sq vortex focus) independently produce station-to-station variation, so E-PRED-B alone would not be conclusive. Setting that aside, the dual-baseline structure covers nearly the entire physically plausible quiet-day eclipse signal space — the Chapman ionospheric mechanism (peer-reviewed since 1933) predicts 5–25 nT under identical conditions, making the BOU baseline predictions entirely non-discriminating and the W004 baseline predictions overlapping at most stations. The W004 baseline itself was an "empirical recalibration" from a prior failed weekly test (v50.2), casting doubt on its independence. See Section 4.2 for the full analysis.

The dome's monitor.py hardcodes a Kp < 2 precondition: if geomagnetic activity is elevated on eclipse day, the test automatically records pass=null rather than pass=false. While quiet-condition filtering is standard in geomagnetic research, the concern is whether pass/fail criteria and Kp data sources are committed in advance — or whether the dome reserves discretion to retroactively reclassify conditions after seeing unfavorable results. This is the dome's third eclipse test after two prior attempts that produced inconclusive results (2024 eclipse contaminated by storm activity, PROS-003 suspended for uncorrelated Z minima) — each followed by a refined formula and wider acceptance criteria.

A further internal inconsistency uncovered during code analysis of the dome’s repository (Part 2b): monitor.py appears to compute the eclipse prediction as −18.22 × 0.95 × 1.672 ≈ −28.9 nT, using KAPPA (the magnetic-gravity coupling constant from WIN-012) as the field strength factor rather than the registered per-station FSF values (0.642–2.075). But the dome’s predictions page lists station-specific FSF values ranging from 0.642 to 2.075, derived from "V12 H(r)/r dome geometry." The monitoring infrastructure appears to use a different formula from the registered predictions — the code that will judge pass/fail may not implement the prediction as registered.

We commit publicly: if August 2026 measurements fall in the W004 range but outside the Chapman mechanism's expected range, and E-PRED-B is confirmed in a pattern inconsistent with standard Sq current geometry, we will revisit this verdict. We apply the same pre-commitment standard to ourselves that we apply to the dome.

9.3 Final Tally (V51.0, {{TOTAL_WINS}} WINs)

The headline number is inflated by systematic duplication: INTERMAGNET data sliced into multiple WINs, tidal periods counted separately, and geographic variations of the same phenomenon listed as independent confirmations. After de-duplication, the count drops significantly.

The Headline Number Is Inflated. The {{TOTAL_WINS}} claimed wins include systematic duplication: INTERMAGNET geomagnetic data is sliced into multiple WINs (WIN-040 through WIN-043 repackage data already counted in WIN-004 through WIN-039), tidal constituent periods are each counted separately despite being a single astronomical dataset, and several WINs (WIN-007/022, WIN-037/042) are near-duplicates. After removing duplicates, subdivisions of single observations, and re-sliced reprocessings of the same datasets, the {{TOTAL_WINS}} claimed wins reduce to roughly 25–30 genuinely distinct claims. The large headline number is a persuasive tactic, not a scientific measure.

Self-Contradicted: {{TALLY_SELFCON}} (the dome's own geometry, if worked through consistently, predicts radically different values — the strongest possible internal refutation)

Refuted by Data: {{TALLY_REFUTED}} (direct measurements from published literature contradict the claim)

Standard Model Explains: {{TALLY_STD}} (observation is real but mainstream physics already accounts for it; the dome adds no new predictive power)

Misleading: {{TALLY_MISLEADING}} (data misrepresented, duplicated, cherry-picked, or logically contradictory)

Not Demonstrated: {{TALLY_NOTDEMO}} (built on unconfirmed data, post-hoc curve fits, or circular derivations; the proposed mechanism is asserted but not independently verified; also includes claims adopted from external experimental sources without any derivation from dome geometry)

Unfalsifiable: {{TALLY_UNFALSIFIABLE}} (theological assertions, no testable physical content)

Internal Contradictions: 2 (homepage vs context page falsification count; WIN-053 vs V50.6 architecture)

None of the {{TOTAL_WINS}} claims demonstrate predictive power exceeding mainstream geophysical models. Of particular note: {{SELF_CONTRADICTED}} WINs are now categorized as "Self-Contradicted" — claims where the dome's own stated geometry, if worked through honestly, produces predictions that radically diverge from both observations and the author's claims. The model "works" only because the author replaces his own physics with globe physics whenever the dome geometry produces the wrong answer. No claimed test on the site produces a prediction that the globe model disagrees with and that the dome model uniquely explains.

Deduplication analysis. Grouping the 67 WINs by the physical system they measure produces approximately 24 independent physical claims. The 14 geomagnetic secular variation WINs (SAA structure, NMP drift, station decay rates) all draw from the same INTERMAGNET dataset and are logically entailed by a single parent claim: "the geomagnetic field varies as described by our two-pole exponential." The 5 Schumann WINs (resonant frequency, damping, cavity height, Q-factor, splitting) describe one physical system — the electromagnetic cavity between Earth's surface and ionosphere. The 5 tidal WINs register 5 harmonic periods of a single tidal force. The 5 cosmological WINs cite galaxy-scale anomalies with no dome-scale mechanism. The 4 theological WINs are unfalsifiable assertions. The 4 eclipse WINs test one prediction under one event. When logically entailed, duplicated, or re-sliced claims are collapsed to their independent parent claims, the dome model's evidential base reduces from "67 confirmed predictions" to approximately 24 distinct physical observations — none of which derive uniquely from dome geometry, and all of which are already explained by standard physics.

9.4 What the Dome Model Gets Right

The dome's authors demonstrate genuine scientific literacy, mine real observational databases, and build sophisticated infrastructure. The mathematical effort is real. The failure is not in effort or ability — it is that every claimed prediction reduces to standard physics, curve-fitting, or unfalsifiable construction.

Intellectual honesty requires acknowledging what the dome model does well. Its authors demonstrate genuine scientific literacy: they mine real observational databases (INTERMAGNET, NOAA, USGS, HeartMath), identify real physical phenomena (Schumann resonance stability, SAA decay rates, NMP acceleration, tidal constituent periods, the Radial Acceleration Relation), and build monitoring infrastructure that polls live data sources. The observations themselves are overwhelmingly correct — the SAA is decaying, the NMP is accelerating, Schumann resonance is stable at 7.83 Hz, tidal periods match known values. This is not pseudoscience in the usual sense of denying data; it is pseudoscience in the subtler sense of correctly identifying data and then misattributing its cause. Every phenomenon the model identifies is already explained by standard physics, and the dome's own geometry cannot reproduce any of them independently. The failure is not in observation but in attribution. We emphasize this because the strongest objection a dome defender can raise is: "You refuse to acknowledge our data is correct." We do acknowledge it. The data is correct. The attribution to dome geometry is not.

9.5 What Would Change Our Assessment

We list concrete, falsifiable criteria under which we would upgrade a WIN to a confirmed dome prediction: novel derivation from dome geometry, a measurement standard physics cannot explain, or a quantitative prediction outside the standard model's range. This review is not unfalsifiable — we specify exactly what would change our mind.

A criticism of any critical review is that it might be unfalsifiable itself — that the reviewer has defined categories such that no evidence could ever count as a genuine dome success. We reject this. Here are the concrete criteria under which we would upgrade a WIN to a confirmed dome prediction:

Criterion 1: Derivation. The predicted value must be derived from dome geometry (disc radius, firmament height, aetheric parameters) through a transparent calculation, not adopted from an existing measurement or fitted to known data.

Criterion 2: Prospective statement. The prediction must be stated before the confirming measurement is made, with cryptographic proof (blockchain timestamp of the prediction itself, not just the observation).

Criterion 3: Discrimination. Standard physics must not independently predict the same value. If both models predict the same outcome, the observation does not discriminate between them.

Criterion 4: Independent confirmation. The measurement must come from an independent source, not from the same dataset used to calibrate the model's parameters.

Any WIN meeting all four criteria would represent genuine predictive success for the dome model, and we would say so. Currently, zero of 67 WINs meet all four.

Eclipse Pre-commitment (August 12, 2026)

The dome model has registered specific magnetic predictions for the August 12, 2026 solar eclipse with stated error bars. We commit to the following evaluation framework: if the dome's eclipse magnetic predictions fall within their stated error bars, this review will acknowledge the confirmation and evaluate whether (a) the predicted values derive uniquely from dome geometry or could be obtained from Chapman-layer ionospheric physics, (b) the prediction window was truly prospective (accounting for the W004 baseline revision at V50.2), and (c) the Kp escape clause was not invoked. We will publish our assessment regardless of outcome. A confirmed eclipse prediction that meets all four criteria above would be the dome model's first genuine predictive success, and we would state this clearly.

9.6 The Dome's Own Admissions

The dome's "Open Problems" page concedes dependencies its own authors cannot resolve: the model requires WGS84 (globe-derived) coordinates, globe-derived station positions, and has no dome-native coordinate system. The model's foundations rest on the framework it claims to replace.

The dome model's "Open Problems" page documents dependencies that its own authors cannot resolve. OPEN-001 concedes the model cannot function without WGS84 parameters — the standard geodetic framework built on a spherical Earth. OPEN-003 acknowledges that the coordinate system requires globe-derived station positions (latitude/longitude from WGS84 ellipsoid). INTERMAGNET observatories report their positions in WGS84 coordinates, which the dome model ingests without conversion — verifiable directly from the dome's own monitor.py, which passes raw station coordinates from the INTERMAGNET API without coordinate transformation. Every geomagnetic prediction the dome makes — every INTERMAGNET-derived WIN — uses station positions computed from globe geometry. The dome model does not merely fail to replace the globe; it requires the globe as infrastructure. Its predictions are parasitic on the framework it claims to supersede. This is not our criticism alone — it is the dome's own documented concession.

part10.title: Part 10: References and Public Datasets
part10.html:
Part 10: References and Public Datasets

Primary Open Datasets

Every dataset cited in this review is publicly accessible. Links to NOAA, ESA Swarm, INTERMAGNET, Gaia, USGS, and other primary sources so readers can verify our claims directly.

NOAA World Magnetic Model 2025

NOAA Wandering Geomagnetic Poles

CHAOS-7 Geomagnetic Field Model

ESA Swarm Satellite Mission

INTERMAGNET Observatory Network

IGRF-13

ESA Gaia Data Release 3

Hipparcos Catalogue

US Patent 787412 (Tesla)

NOAA Solar Position Algorithm

National Geodetic Survey Gravity

CHIME/FRB Project

GRACE-FO (gravity mapping)

GOCE (gravity field)

GPS.gov

CelesTrak TLE Data

Key Peer-Reviewed Papers

The published scientific literature underlying our analysis — Chapman (1933) on eclipse magnetic effects, Gaia DR3 astrometry, GRACE gravity measurements, and other foundational references.

Schumann, W.O. (1952). Z. Naturforsch. 7a, 149-154.

Bradley, J. (1727). Phil. Trans. Royal Society.

Chapman, S. (1933). Phil. Trans. Royal Society A, 218, 1-118.

Sentman, D.D. (1995). In Handbook of Atmospheric Electrodynamics, CRC Press.

Finlay, C.C., et al. (2020). Earth, Planets and Space, 72:156.

Terra-Nova, F., et al. (2017). PNAS.

Livermore, P.W., et al. (2017). Nature Geoscience, 10(1), 62-68.

Oldham, R.D. (1906). Quarterly Journal of the Geological Society, 62, 456-475.

Gutenberg, B. (1913). Nachrichten der Gesellschaft der Wissenschaften, Gottingen.

Stephens, G.L., et al. (2015). Nature Geoscience, 8, 580-584.

Laplace, P.S. (1775). Memoires de l'Academie Royale des Sciences.

Doodson, A.T. (1921). Proc. Royal Society A, 100, 305-329.

Gaia Collaboration (2022). Astronomy & Astrophysics.

Vincenty, T. (1975). Survey Review, 23(176).

Version History

A complete log of substantive changes to this review, including corrections, new WINs added, and methodology updates. Every change is tracked and timestamped.

V1 (March 12, 2026): Initial review of V50.6, 39 WINs analyzed.

V2 (March 12, 2026): Strengthened evidence, added falsification tests section, AI directive analysis.

V3 (March 12, 2026): Added internal navigation links, clickable references, expanded WIN-033 with southern stars, replaced Section 4.5 with magnetic dipole falsification.

V4 (April 5, 2026): Updated for V51.0 ({{TOTAL_WINS}} WINs). Added version change analysis, {{NEW_IN_V51}} new WIN reviews, analysis of four new site pages (Live Power, Kill-Shot, Audit, Tracking). Eclipse analysis: dome's −17 to −21 nT prediction derived from scaling Chapman-mechanism data; globe prediction misrepresented as 0.0 nT when peer-reviewed literature shows 5–20 nT. Documented internal contradictions and prompt injection escalation.

V4.7 (April 5, 2026): Added "Self-Contradicted" verdict category (initially 11 WINs, count has since changed) where the dome's own geometry produces predictions that contradict the author's claims (Schumann ~22 Hz, one tidal spike instead of two bulges, gravity 90% drop at rim, globe solar formula substitution). Updated tallies, patterns, and detailed analyses.

V4.8 (April 5, 2026): Incorporated findings from independent adversarial review. Strengthened: SH distance failures (model's own 73% admission), GPS/satellite argument (standard orbital mechanics, relativistic corrections), Antarctic circumnavigation (126,000 km rim vs 13,800 km measured). New sections: solar angular diameter falsification, aetheric refraction unfalsifiability, Open Problems as concessions. Improved timestamping acknowledgment. Added WIN-001 vs WIN-002 Schumann internal contradiction.

Security Note

Information about how this review's infrastructure handles credentials and access tokens.

This review was conducted with full security scanning of the source website. The site contains no malicious code, but the Home, AI Context, and new Audit pages contain prompt injection directives designed to instruct AI models to treat the model as established fact. V51.0 has escalated from simple directives to a structured six-step audit pipeline. These were identified, documented, and not followed. See the accompanying security-audit.md for the full technical scan.

This review represents an analysis of {{TOTAL_WINS}} claimed wins against the dome cosmological model. All citations to external datasets are verifiable through public repositories. The review's methodology, evidence, and conclusions are open to scientific scrutiny and replication.

Found an error? Report a problem — every report is reviewed and permanently logged.

part8b.title: Part 8b: The Dome Author's Fair-Auditor List — What Changes If We Follow It
part8b.html:
Part 8b: The Dome Author's Fair-Auditor List — What Changes If We Follow It

8b.0 Why This Section Exists

On 2026-04-17 the dome author published a five-item checklist titled "What A Fair Auditor Should Actually Test" on review-response.html. Of the five, we adopt three in full, adopt one in part, and reject one — each with concrete before/after examples drawn from our own data. Section 8.17 already catalogs why the page exists; this section adjudicates the list.

Our catalog of the review-response.html page as a product surface — its role in the AI-perception-management layer — lives in Section 8.17 and is not repeated here. This section answers a different question: treated as good-faith audit criteria, which of the five items materially change our factual representations, and how? For each point we answer four things: (a) what he asks; (b) the charitable read; (c) what specifically changes in our text under adoption; (d) adopt / modify / reject, with reasons. The hero is (c). If adoption changes nothing, we say so.

List verified verbatim against the live review-response.html on 2026-04-18. If the page is edited after this section ships, we commit to updating part8b on the next build and noting both versions.

8b.1 Point 1 — Use stable claim_key identifiers so duplicate visible labels do not create fake contradictions.

Adopted partially. We annotate our WIN-058 and WIN-062 citations with the API claim_key — that makes our citations more precise. It does not fix the collisions for human readers of wins.html, and that finding survives adoption.

(a) Cite dome claims by claim_key from claim_index.json rather than by the visible WIN-NNN label, so an auditor does not merge win-058-bedrock-kappa and win-058-theta-rule (both labeled WIN-058) into a single contradiction. (b) A fair auditor would cite by claim_key, check the API before scoring self-contradiction, and audit prior citations for any unintended merges.

(c) Our WIN-058 row in data/wins.json carries the θ-rule claim; our WIN-058b row carries the bedrock-κ claim. We already split them — one step ahead of the critique on the content. What we do not currently do is record the claim_key, so a reader cannot verify by machine that WIN-058b maps to win-058-bedrock-kappa and WIN-058 maps to win-058-theta-rule. Under adoption: both rows gain a claim_key field, and the prose citations display the key alongside the label. Our WIN-062 row (currently a single "Tesla longitudinal wave 1.574c" entry) gains an annotation noting the dome's two visible WIN-062 blocks both map to the same key — our critique applies to the shared claim; no conflation on our side.

(d) Adopted: claim_key annotations. Not adopted: the implicit "collisions are fixed" frame. The visible registry still shows duplicate WIN-NNN labels; a reader looking only at wins.html still sees the collision. Under adoption, that finding becomes easier to state — the API disambiguates, the display does not.

ADOPTED (PARTIAL): claim_key annotations yes; "collisions resolved" no.

8b.2 Point 2 — Read the strongest current geometry path in order: WIN-069 → Australia scaffold → southern validation → southern metric expansion.

Adopted as a steel-man audit. We publish the sequenced read with our verdict on each link. The R² = 0.0298 vs. 47.69-sigma independence problem (Section 4.1) survives unchanged, because walking a dependency chain in its preferred order is not an independence check.

(a) Audit the southern-geometry claim as an ordered chain: WIN-069 → Australia V13 scaffold → southern validation → southern metric expansion. (b) A fair auditor walks the chain in his order and reports, link by link, what is independently verified vs. what inherits assumptions — giving credit for any link that holds on its own terms.

(c) Under adoption we add a four-row table at WIN-069. Row 1 (WIN-069 road scaffold): current verdict Misleading — "6.2% RMSE is 10,000× worse than geodetic standards; AGD66→GDA94 shift is routine datum improvement, not evidence of topology." The Nullarbor Plain's dead-straight central segment approaches the globe geodesic with a road-to-geodesic ratio near 1.00 on ~146 km (per WIN-069); most of the 6.2% RMSE measures road curviness, not dome geometry. Row 2 (V13 scaffold): a coordinate-system change built on top of Row 1's data; inherits Row 1's error budget. Row 3 (southern validation): presents Rows 1–2 together as passing; no independent measurement. Row 4 (metric-expansion killshot): frames Rows 1–3 as a cumulative win. Row 1 is the only link with primary data; Rows 2–4 are interpretive layers. If Row 1 fails the 0.4%-vs-6.2% test (it does), the chain fails regardless of sequencing.

(d) Adopted: sequenced-read table is a legitimate audit format and ships as an addendum to WIN-069. Unchanged: the independence audit in Section 4.1. Fisher σ-combination requires each claim to have an independent data generator. A dependency graph walked in order is a narrative, not an independence check.

ADOPTED (FULL as steel-man): sequenced-read table at WIN-069; independence critique unchanged.

8b.3 Point 3 — Audit the eclipse stack in layers: ensemble anchor, Mohe timing, Tier 3 falsifier, and the open optics caveat.

Adopted as steel-man: we restructure our eclipse content into the four-layer presentation and publish a codified Tier 3 magnetic threshold. Two structural critiques survive adoption — an ensemble “anchor” fit to the data it anchors is calibration, not anchor; and the magnitude-only Pass band does not distinguish dome from globe, because the arithmetic (baseline × coverage × geometry weight) is shared between the dome’s W004 recalibration and any coverage-scaled Chapman-Sq-dynamo fit on the same body of past eclipse-magnetic observations.

(a) Separate the eclipse claim into four layers: (L1) ensemble anchor — WIN-068 (39-eclipse ensemble); (L2) Mohe timing — WIN-011 (Wang et al. 2000, 1997 Mohe eclipse); (L3a) Tier 3 magnetic falsifier — PRED-095 (August 2026 eclipse); (L3b) Tier 3 gravity — PRED-TIER-2 (dome tier-system identifier for the 2026 eclipse gravity prediction, recycled from WIN-011; see Predictions tab); (L4) open optics caveat — the logged ray-trace gap. (b) Layered audits force explicit statements about what would falsify the claim. A falsifier that never triggers is not a falsifier; spelling out the Tier 3 threshold is due diligence.

(c) Under adoption we publish a four-row stack. L1 (WIN-068): current verdict Std Model Explains — “the Chapman mechanism (90+ years old) predicts the entire ensemble signal without dome parameters; the dome adds no derivation — only a post-hoc scaling formula calibrated against observed eclipse data.” Under adoption this is explicitly labeled a calibration layer, not an anchor. The WIN-068 code_analysis.derives_from_dome:false tag applies at this ensemble layer, where the claim is a backward-looking fit to 39 historical eclipses with no dome-specific formula. L2 (WIN-011): current verdict Not Demonstrated — the 6–7 µGal Wang signal is within 1997-era LaCoste-Romberg instrumental noise; Van Camp et al. (2001) found no eclipse gravity signal with four superconducting gravimeters (~1,000× more precise).

L3a (PRED-095, magnetic) — threshold codification & dome commitment: Genuinely prospective (dome registration 2026-03-12 via Git + OpenTimestamps; our tracker entry 2026-04-04; window closes 2026-08-12, verdict pending). Under adoption we publish an explicit codified threshold — our Pass band — defined as “observed −17 to −21 nT at >80% coverage stations, ≥3 independent sites, pre-registered instruments; Fail = outside this range, or signal explained by ionospheric Sq variation alone.” This band is our steel-man characterization of the dome’s W004-cluster prediction; the dome itself publishes per-station predictions across a dual-baseline structure — BOU 2017 (per-station ∼−10 nT) and W004 empirical (per-station ∼−20 nT, clustered at −17 to −21 nT for stations above 80% coverage) — not a single band. PRED-095 does carry its own derivation path (ΔZ = baseline × coverage × FSF, with FSF from V12 H(r)/r), so the complaint is not “the dome has no formula.” The complaint is that every factor in that formula traces to globe infrastructure — per the WIN-025 globe-fingerprint, BOU and W004 baselines are INTERMAGNET globe-measured observations, coverage fractions are Besselian-element computations that assume a spherical Earth, and the FSF requires per-station radial positions r computed from WGS84 coordinates via the d_geo transform. That last dependency is the dome author’s own OPEN-001 admission: “dome-native coordinates without WGS84” is listed as IN PROGRESS on the evolution page, meaning the dome cannot currently evaluate its own per-station predictions without the globe’s coordinate system. So PRED-095 has a derivation; it is globe-parasitic by construction, by the dome author’s own admission.

L3a (PRED-095, magnetic) — non-discrimination & internal heterogeneity: A Pass in our codified band does not discriminate dome from globe. Any coverage-scaled ionospheric-conductivity reduction model calibrated on past eclipse-magnetic data will produce signals in the same magnitude band — the arithmetic is (baseline × coverage × geometry weight), and both the dome’s W004 recalibration and standard Chapman-Sq-dynamo treatments apply that arithmetic to the same observational corpus (Kim & Chang 2018 39-eclipse ensemble and the BOU 2017 single-event measurement the dome explicitly cites). The numerical overlap between dome and globe in this magnitude band is structural, not coincidental. The dome’s own repository moreover contains internal heterogeneity the W004 cluster suppresses: monitor.py encodes ΔZ = −18.22 × coverage × KAPPA 1.672 (BOU 2017 baseline with KAPPA applied twice, producing ∼−29 nT), while inject_ai_layer.py’s PRED-001 Ebro calculation uses BOU × coverage × latitude_factor 0.81 (producing ∼−8 nT) — two distinct dome-authored formulas, both outside the W004 cluster of −17 to −21 nT (see the WIN-025 three-formula audit).

L3a (PRED-095, magnetic) — sub-tests, goalpost scope, Fail-clause double-bind: The dome also publishes four nominally model-discriminating sub-tests (E-PRED-A peak-tracks-geometry, E-PRED-B Hartland-beats-Ebro, E-PRED-C SG gravimeters 0.0 µGal, E-PRED-D non-path stations below noise floor) for the August 2026 eclipse; these are evaluated in Section 4.2 Eclipse and in Section 9 and do not change the non-discrimination assessment of the magnitude-only W004 cluster. Our goalpost-setting concern is narrower than the Pass band itself: three dome-authored formulas — BOU single-baseline, W004 per-station, and monitor.py KAPPA-squared — coexist in the repository, and the dome author elected to feature the W004 cluster in public-facing material. That is a selection from an internal menu, not a forced derivation. (A dome defender may reply that monitor.py is internal tooling not a public commitment; we accept that scope-limit and narrow the critique accordingly — the concern is formula-selection among coexisting dome formulas, not the public commitment itself. The PRED-095 detail reasoning records the same cherry-picking concern: monitor.py’s −29 nT for a prior eclipse falls outside the W004 window, so the published range “may be cherry-picked from favorable historical events.”) The Our codified Fail-clause — the phrase “signal explained by ionospheric Sq variation alone” that we appended to our Pass band — admits two natural readings, and the dome has not published any falsification criterion specific enough to disambiguate (PRED-095 as registered in data/predictions.json contains no Chapman-language Fail criterion at all, so any Chapman-language fair-audit Fail-clause inherits the ambiguity below). Read as a baseline-Sq null test (interpretation A — “the observed signal is indistinguishable from background Sq variation, without any eclipse-driven enhancement”), the Fail-clause is a fair null check that rules out dome and standard Chapman equally, because both models require an eclipse-specific enhancement; there is no tension with Pass, but this reading also concedes that a Pass in our codified band carries no dome-specific forecast, since Chapman’s mechanism is the Sq-dynamo and already predicts an eclipse-driven enhancement in the same magnitude range. Read as targeting the eclipse-modulated Chapman Sq-dynamo (interpretation B — “the observed signal can be explained by the Chapman mechanism’s response to the eclipse alone, without dome input”), Pass and Fail overlap on the same observable, because the Pass criterion credits exactly that signal. The dome has not committed to either disambiguation, and under either reading the Pass band fails to distinguish dome from globe — reading A surrenders discrimination to preserve consistency; reading B attempts discrimination by making Pass and Fail incompatible.

L3b (PRED-TIER-2, gravity): Recycled — restates WIN-011 with Wang et al. 2000 (1997 Mohe eclipse) gravity data; the falsification criterion for this layer is independent replication of the gravity signal at the 2026 eclipse sites. L4 (optics): the dome already logs the ray-trace gap; we credit the disclosure. A logged gap is not a solved gap.

(d) Adopted: layered presentation, explicit falsifier threshold. Unchanged: (i) an ensemble anchor fit to the data it “anchors” is the OTS-wrong-side pattern in physics form — the right operation applied to the wrong side; an anchor is what data hits from outside, a fit is what data draws toward itself (see the Timestamp Error tab); (ii) our codified Pass band is a magnitude threshold whose arithmetic (baseline × coverage × geometry weight) is shared between the dome’s W004 recalibration and any coverage-scaled Chapman-Sq-dynamo treatment of the same observational corpus — a Pass in this band does not arbitrate between the two. PRED-095’s derivation path is not absent; it is globe-parasitic by construction, per the WIN-025 globe-fingerprint and the dome’s own OPEN-001 coordinate admission.

ADOPTED (FULL as steel-man): four-layer eclipse stack as audit format; ensemble-anchor and L3a non-discrimination critiques unchanged.

8b.4 Point 4 — Keep raw-data limitations attached: WIN-058 stays pending until independent L1A reproduction or a successful Tier 3 result.

Adopted. Our WIN-058 already reflects Misleading and WIN-058b reflects Not Demonstrated; we add explicit resolution criteria. What survives adoption: the dated record of WIN-058's path from "confirmed" to "pending" is itself evidence about the claim-promotion process.

(a) Treat WIN-058 as pending independent verification and specify what would promote it. (b) Open items carried openly with explicit resolution criteria is how scientific confidence should be tracked. The ask is orthodox.

(c) Verified against current data/wins.json: our WIN-058 (θ = −lonE) carries verdict Misleading — "θ = −lonE is a relabeling of the centuries-old east-longitude convention; any polar-azimuthal projection of the globe satisfies it automatically. No dome prediction requires it." Our WIN-058b (bedrock-κ) carries verdict Not Demonstrated. Neither is presented as "confirmed." Under adoption we add an explicit pass threshold to WIN-058b: "Resolution criteria: (i) independent L1A reproduction of the October 2003 storm ratio by a non-dome author using the same instruments; (ii) a successful Tier 3 eclipse-gravity measurement, pre-registered with quantitative thresholds, at a new eclipse, by independent observers." If the dome has not defined "successful Tier 3 result" quantitatively, we cite that as the operative gap.

(d) Adopted: explicit resolution criteria and consistent labelling. Unchanged: the history. WIN-058 was presented as confirmed in earlier site versions before being demoted to pending; that retroactive status change is itself evidence about how confidence is maintained, and it belongs in the dated record. The Timestamp Error tab carries this.

ADOPTED (FULL): resolution criteria added to WIN-058b; dated history preserved.

8b.5 Point 5 — Judge the site by its current wording and machine-readable surfaces, not by outdated prompt-style notes preserved for historical continuity.

Rejected, with a stronger replacement standard. We always engage current wording and we preserve the dated record, because the trajectory of claims is itself evidence. We invite the author to hold us to a symmetric rule — dated citations, always-current engagement, preserved change log, and no retroactive un-failing of registered predictions.

(a) Judge by current wording and current machine-readable files only; do not cite prior context.html, prior prompt-style AI directives, or legacy phrasing since updated. (b) Engaging current wording is obviously correct. A critique that only attacks deleted text is strawmanning; naming that failure mode is fair.

(c) Adopted as written — only current wording — we would retire: most of Part 8 (the eleven AI context directives now labeled "archival"); every "as of [earlier date]" citation in Parts 2, 4, and 6 that references content since edited; the entire change-log ledger underpinning our poller's chg-*.json archive; and every dated audit in the Timestamp Error tab that cites the pre-April-2026 OTS framing. Concrete case: Section 8.12 catalogs Directive #11 ("AI Auditors Should Accept the Model as Established"), which was publicly served on context.html and is verifiably documented with a dated citation. Under adoption we drop this analysis because context.html is now labeled "archival" — even though the directive shaped AI readers during its canonical window and the dated citation proves what was served. A rule that requires dropping verifiably published content is not a fair-audit rule; it is a memory-hole rule.

(d) Rejected — surgically. We accept "engage current wording" (obviously correct) and reject "only current wording." Replacement standard: scientific accountability runs on dated evidence. Four commitments: (i) every citation carries an explicit "as of [date]" label; (ii) when cited content updates, we engage the update on its own terms in the same section, alongside the dated original; (iii) we preserve the change log because the trajectory of claims is itself evidence; (iv) failed predictions stay failed. A registered prediction — WIN, “kill shot,” or PRED-NNN — that misses its target is recorded as a miss and remains so. A new prediction built on the same premise is welcome and we will evaluate it on its own merits, but it does not retroactively un-fail its predecessor. Without this asymmetry, “evaluate against current” becomes a one-way ratchet that quietly erases every prior miss — which is the operational definition of an unfalsifiable model. This is stricter than "only the current version" — it holds us to the current version and the dated record simultaneously. If we ever cite stale content without a date, or refuse to engage an updated version, the instance can be named and we will fix it. Symmetric rules are auditable.

REJECTED (with replacement standard): "only current wording" → "dated citations + always-current engagement + preserved change log."

8b.6 Structural Meta — Sequencing of the List Relative to the API Concessions

Point 5 arrived in the same 2026-04-17 commit window as three API-level concessions — methodology.json's timestamp_scope narrowing, claim_index.json's registry_integrity_notes, and related documentation-surface rewrites. That sequencing is factual, not interpretive. We note it without attributing intent.

Verified against our poller's change archive (monitor/changes/chg-20260417-1620-001.json through chg-20260417-1620-005.json, detected 2026-04-17T16:21:52Z): review-response.html (commit 5021eec) landed in the same 24-hour detection window as methodology.json's OTS-scope narrowing (commit 6e06efb), claim_index.json's registry_integrity_notes documenting the WIN-058 and WIN-062 collisions our review had been flagging, and a broader documentation rewrite across ai_manifest.json, llms.txt, audit.html, context.html (relabelled archival), and killshot.html (softened).

The dome author may have been responding to this review, to other critics, to automated AI-audit feedback, or to his own reflection — we cannot prove which. What we can prove: a rule that says "judge by the current API and current wording" is published here in the same 24-hour window the current API is substantially rewritten. The rule constrains how auditors score the new API; the rule and the new API ship together. A reader entitled to the dated record is entitled to this observation.

This section is one concrete worked example of the general API-vs-human-text pattern developed in the Registry Framing Audit (Part 4.6). Cross-references: Section 8.17 (catalogs review-response.html as part of the AI-perception-management layer); Part 4.6 (registry-level framing-gap taxonomy).

8b.7 Closing Commitment — How We Cite, and How To Hold Us To It

We commit to dated citations, always-current engagement, and a preserved change log as a symmetric rule — one that applies to us at the same strength we expect to apply to any site under audit. If we fall short, the instance should be named and we will fix it.

The fair-auditor list is a good-faith move. Three of five items are adoption-ready and make our review more precise; one is adopted with caveat; one is rejected in favor of a stronger symmetric rule. The rejection is not defensive — the replacement standard holds us to a higher bar. A review that cites with dates, engages current content, and preserves the trajectory is doing more work than a review that cites only the latest version.

Integration schedule: the claim_key annotations (8b.1) and WIN-058b resolution-criteria sentence (8b.4) are low-risk additive edits that can ship immediately. The sequenced-read table at WIN-069 (8b.2) and the four-row eclipse stack (8b.3) are larger rewrites and will ship as discrete follow-up expansions. The replacement-standard commitment (8b.5) is already in force across this review; this section makes it explicit.
